name: Alibi Explain (Model Explainability)

performance.notes: Explanation algorithms vary widely; counterfactuals and anchors can be slow

agent_readiness.notes: ML explainability algorithms for tabular, image, text. from alibi.explainers import AnchorTabular. explainer = AnchorTabular(predict_fn, feature_names). explainer.fit(X_train). exp = explainer.explain(X_test[0]). exp.anchor → rule-based explanation ('feature > 0.5 AND feature2 = cat'). CounterFactual: from alibi.explainers import Counterfactual. cf = Counterfactual(model, shape). exp = cf.explain(X). exp.cf['X'] → closest counterfactual. SHAP TreeExplainer, KernelExplainer wrappers. IntegratedGradients for TF models. Integrated Gradients. CEM (Contrastive Explanation Method). PartialDependence. Trust Scores.

gotchas: Counterfactual search is computationally expensive — may time out
Anchor explanations require fitting on training data — not fully model-agnostic
explanations.meta and explanations.data structure is non-trivial to navigate
best_when: Anchor (rule-based) explanations: 'why was this prediction made?'
Counterfactual explanations: 'what minimal change would flip the prediction?'
Contrastive Explanation Method (CEM) for neural networks
When production ML serving (Seldon) needs explanation APIs
avoid_when: Simple SHAP values (use standalone shap library)
Real-time explanation serving at scale (expensive algorithms)
summary: Alibi provides ML explanations including Anchors, Counterfactuals, CEM, SHAP. AnchorTabular.explain(X) → rule-based 'IF feature > 0.5 THEN class=1'. Counterfactual.explain(X) → minimal flip. IntegratedGradients for TF. Apache-2.0 by Seldon. Best for anchors and counterfactuals. Counterfactual search is slow. Some methods need TF. Integrates with Seldon production serving.
